question: What was our revenue last month?
sql: SELECT ROUND(SUM(sale_price), 2) AS revenue FROM `bigquery-public-data.thelook_ecommerce.order_items` WHERE status IN ('Complete', 'Shipped') AND created_at >= TIMESTAMP(DATE_TRUNC(DATE_SUB(CURRENT_DATE(), INTERVAL 1 MONTH), MONTH)) AND created_at < TIMESTAMP(DATE_TRUNC(CURRENT_DATE(), MONTH))
analyst_notes: Revenue counts only items with status Complete or Shipped. Cancelled and Returned items are excluded (they inflate the naive sum by roughly a quarter). Processing items count only toward 'projected revenue' and must be labeled as such when included.

question: Who are our top 10 customers by total spend?
sql: SELECT u.id AS customer_id, CONCAT(u.first_name, ' ', u.last_name) AS customer, u.state, ROUND(SUM(oi.sale_price), 2) AS total_spend, COUNT(DISTINCT oi.order_id) AS orders FROM `bigquery-public-data.thelook_ecommerce.order_items` oi JOIN `bigquery-public-data.thelook_ecommerce.users` u ON u.id = oi.user_id WHERE oi.status IN ('Complete', 'Shipped') GROUP BY 1, 2, 3 ORDER BY total_spend DESC LIMIT 10
analyst_notes: Identify customers by id and name, never by email or address. Spend uses the standard revenue definition (Complete + Shipped). Order count alongside spend distinguishes whales from frequent mid-size buyers.

question: Which states have the highest revenue?
sql: SELECT u.state, ROUND(SUM(oi.sale_price), 2) AS revenue FROM `bigquery-public-data.thelook_ecommerce.order_items` oi JOIN `bigquery-public-data.thelook_ecommerce.users` u ON u.id = oi.user_id WHERE oi.status IN ('Complete', 'Shipped') AND u.country = 'United States' GROUP BY u.state ORDER BY revenue DESC LIMIT 10
analyst_notes: users.state is global — it holds provinces and regions from every market (Guangdong, England, Sao Paulo...). For 'state' questions default to country = 'United States' and say so; only go global when the user asks. Note country spellings are literal strings: 'United States', 'Brasil'.

question: Why are users in Texas underspending compared to California?
sql: SELECT u.state, ROUND(SUM(oi.sale_price) / COUNT(DISTINCT u.id), 2) AS spend_per_user, ROUND(COUNT(DISTINCT oi.order_id) / COUNT(DISTINCT u.id), 2) AS orders_per_user, ROUND(SUM(oi.sale_price) / COUNT(DISTINCT oi.order_id), 2) AS avg_order_value FROM `bigquery-public-data.thelook_ecommerce.users` u LEFT JOIN `bigquery-public-data.thelook_ecommerce.order_items` oi ON oi.user_id = u.id AND oi.status IN ('Complete', 'Shipped') WHERE u.state IN ('Texas', 'California') GROUP BY u.state
analyst_notes: 'Why' questions are never one query. Decompose the gap: spend per user = order frequency x average order value, so first find WHICH factor drives the difference, then drill into that factor (category mix, age demographics, traffic_source cohorts). Compare per-user rates, never raw state totals - state populations differ. Conclude with the dominant factor and note what was ruled out.
